question: Does the two-level K128 query-major micro-partial producer become a speedup candidate at S4096 layer 2?
short_answer: No. The K128 micro-partial runtime probe preserves the modeled write reduction, but the dense active-group by selected-K route-matrix producer is itself about 58 ms, far slower than the current 8.0-8.8 ms sparse-atomic wall. The next kernel must not use a dense active-group route matrix for microtile reduction.

comparison.target_gate_ms: producer plus merge plus final reducer must beat 8.0-8.8 ms

what_worked: The micro-partial script compiles and passes S128 parity against the PyTorch dKV reference.
The K128 metadata/write-reduction model is reproduced by the runtime probe: 805M raw scalar dKV elements shrink to 126M micro-partial elements.
what_failed: At S4096 layer 2, K128 emit-only is 58.4 ms before any selected-tile merge or final reducer.
The final torch index_add adds only about 0.5 ms on top of K128 emit-only, so the wall is not final reduction. It is the dense active-group route-matrix producer.
interpretation.root_cause: Each S4096 layer-2 microtile still has hundreds to about one thousand active compressed groups. The Triton micro producer forms an active_group x selected_K route matrix and multiplies it into KxD contribution tiles, so it replaces global atomic pressure with much larger route-matrix compute.
interpretation.decision: Retire dense-route micro-partial producer as the next sparse-dKV runtime candidate.

retain: compact final reduce-by-key or segmented reducer as a separate small stage
avoid: dense active_group x selected_K route matrices
micro active-list dot routing with hundreds of active groups
next_kernel_contract.candidate: query-major sparse-keyed segmented reduction: compute KxD contribution tiles once, then use a true sparse keyed local reducer over selected_groups before global writes, without expanding selected groups into a dense active-group route matrix.
